entities.description: The checked-in XML file in the pinned CME submodule, including TEMPHEAD revision metadata and the encoded Horstmann selection.
entities.label: General Corpus repository artifact for CME00147
notes: The root TEXT language string enm lat ger describes the encoded representation, including Latin appendices and German editorial matter, rather than one uniform medieval work language.
The XML preserves 6,738 italic HI spans from Horstmann's typography; their editorial function requires manuscript collation rather than a single global expansion rule.

rights.basis: The pinned CME repository contains an Unlicense text framed as applying to software. Its scope over corpus XML, markup, data, and edition-derived typography is not explicit enough to treat the full artifact as unambiguously licensed.

access.entityLabel: University of Michigan electronic text AJE0623.0001.001 / CME00147

rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the material is in the public domain and supplies No Copyright-United States status.
rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan

entities.description: The U-M Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item encoding Horstmann's title page and text pp. 1-112. The public item identifier is AJE0623.0001.001 while the source XML's IDG, BIBNO, and VID are CME00147.
entities.label: University of Michigan electronic text AJE0623.0001.001 / CME00147
notes: The U-M catalog places Edith, Saint in an author field. The medieval poem is anonymous; Edith is its subject.

The item is complete relative to Horstmann's title page and pp. 1-112 but selective relative to the complete 1883 volume; it stops after seven Auctoritates entries and omits the continuation on p. 113.

rights.basis: The current U-M item states that the material is in the public domain and supplies No Copyright-United States status.
rights.component: Electronic text as distributed by the University of Michigan

dateStatements.label: composition
dateStatements.notes: The accompanying founders list ends with Henry V as the reigning king; the normalized range is an inference, not a dated colophon.
dateStatements.value: around 1420, probably during the reign of Henry V

entities.description: Anonymous Middle English verse hagiography and institutional history composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It integrates Wilton's royal foundations and historical memory with the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith.
entities.label: The Wilton Chronicle
notes: Only one manuscript witness is known.
The author is anonymous and closely associated with Wilton. Chaplain, priest, or nun identities remain hypotheses.
The poem is incomplete because an entire twelve-folio gathering was removed after surviving line 1030.

rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed around 1420 and is centuries outside copyright term. Modern editions, markup, translations, scholarship, and photographs remain separate components.
rights.component: Medieval Middle English literary content

entities.description: The eleventh-century Latin Edith legend associated with Goscelin and treated in modern scholarship as a principal source for the Wilton poem's hagiographic material. The exact recension or physical exemplar used by the Middle English poet has not been identified.
entities.label: Legenda sanctae Edithae
notes: Horstmann questioned whether the short twelve-chapter Vita printed by the Bollandists was identical with the Legenda cited by the poet.

bibliographic.citation: Carl Horstmann, ed., S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III (Heilbronn: Gebr. Henninger, 1883).
bibliographic.title: S. Editha sive Chronicon Vilodunense im Wiltshire Dialekt aus Ms. Cotton. Faustina B III

entities.description: Carl Horstmann's complete scholarly volume, including German introduction, direct transcription of the Cotton witness, extensive critical and source notes, two Latin appendices, remarks, and a second appendix. CME00147 encodes the title page and printed pp. 1-112, stopping partway through Auctoritates, not the complete volume.
entities.label: Horstmann's 1883 edition of S. Editha
notes: Horstmann cites Black's 1830 edition as generally correct but repeatedly reports manuscript readings, corrections, erasures, and differences from Black, demonstrating direct recollation rather than derivation from Black alone.
Printed pp. 111-113 contain the Latin Nomina fundatorum and Auctoritates after the poem.

rights.basis: The volume was published in 1883 and is outside the maximum United States copyright term; Internet Archive metadata marks the item NOT_IN_COPYRIGHT in the United States.
rights.component: 1883 edition, transcription, introduction, notes, and typography

access.accessMethod: No complete public digital copy was located. The University of Leeds Special Collections catalog reports an Archive Print copy at Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL; consult Leeds for onsite access or reproduction possibilities.
access.entityLabel: Black's 1830 first edition of Chronicon Vilodunense

bibliographic.citation: William Henry Black, ed., Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano (London: Typis Nicholsianis, 1830).
bibliographic.title: Chronicon Vilodunense; sive de vita et miraculis Sanctae Edithae Regis Edgari filiae carmen vetus Anglicum, e codice unico Cottoniano

entities.description: William Henry Black's first printed edition of the unique Cotton text, produced at the expense of Richard Colt Hoare. Horstmann describes it as generally correct and uses it as a collation point while returning to the manuscript.
entities.label: Black's 1830 first edition of Chronicon Vilodunense
notes: A current British Library component bibliography gives 1802, but the inspected Horstmann history, DIMEV, title citation, and library holdings support 1830.
Only 100 copies were reportedly printed; no complete public digital scan was located in the searches recorded here.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1830 and its nineteenth-century editor died long ago; the work is outside ordinary copyright terms. Repository or reproduction terms for a particular copy remain separate.
rights.component: 1830 edition and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions with ordinary author-term protection

access.accessMethod: Follow the current British Library Archives and Manuscripts request instructions. The catalog states that a letter of introduction is required and consultation is onsite in London, subject to current conditions.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199r-264v

access.accessMethod: Submit the online digitisation form with shelfmark Cotton MS Faustina B III and exact folios. The service distinguishes reference JPEGs from reproduction-quality TIFFs and asks users to contact licensing for publication reuse.
access.entityLabel: London, British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199r-264v
access.format: Research JPEG or reproduction-quality TIFF quotation
notes: The public IIIF sequence already provides a complete research facsimile. A paid order may be useful only if higher resolution, color control, or publication-quality files are needed.

The rate card lists 2025/26 standard charges of £15.25 for the first JPEG image and £0.58 for each subsequent JPEG; £27.70 for the first TIFF and £7.25 for each subsequent TIFF; complex imaging is £58.19 per image. Obtain a current quotation before relying on these rates.

rights.basis: The imaging service applies its own order terms and directs publication users to licensing. A paid image order does not by itself grant redistribution or publication rights.
rights.component: Newly commissioned research or reproduction-quality images

dateStatements.label: target component production
dateStatements.value: first quarter of the fifteenth century

dateStatements.label: probable copying date in modern scholarship

entities.description: The sole manuscript witness of The Wilton Chronicle in a composite parchment codex. The Middle English poem is now ff. 199r-263r; a Latin Wilton founders account and source list follow on ff. 263r-264v, and the companion Middle English Life of St Æthelthryth follows on ff. 265r-279v. The target component was produced at Wilton in the first quarter of the fifteenth century.

historical_designations: Horstmann's foliation ff. 194-259 for the poem and appendices
holding.notes: Current foliation is five leaves higher than Horstmann's milestones across the target component. The poem is current ff. 199r-263r; Latin appendices current ff. 263r-264v.

entities.label: London, British Library, Cotton MS Faustina B III, ff. 199r-264v

physicalDescription.extent: Composite codex, i + 280 folios; target poem ff. 199r-263r; complete adjacent Latin texts ff. 263r-264v; CME00147's encoded Latin selection ends on f. 264r
notes: The poem has a twelve-folio gathering missing after surviving line 1030, between Horstmann old ff. 205 and 206 / current ff. 210 and 211.
Benskin treats the extant language as mixed and predominantly West Midland, produced by a scribe probably from northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and likely working at Wilton.
The same hand copies the Edith poem, the intervening Latin account, and the Æthelthryth poem; this does not establish autograph status.

rights.basis: The manuscript content is medieval and centuries outside copyright term. Physical ownership, photography, and provider terms are separate.
rights.component: Fifteenth-century manuscript textual and codicological content

rights.basis: The imaging service applies its own order terms and directs publication users to licensing. A paid image order does not by itself grant redistribution or publication rights.
rights.component: Newly commissioned research or reproduction-quality images

next_steps: Ask University of Leeds Special Collections about photography or digitisation of Large Modern History Y-3.1/WIL.
Search Google Books, HathiTrust, British Library, Bodleian, Salisbury Cathedral, and WorldCat holdings by the full Latin title and imprint.
Cache the complete edition if a lawful direct download becomes available.
notes: The edition is extant and requestable through holdings; no-public-copy-found is only a digital-access finding.
openQuestions.question: Does a complete public digital facsimile of William Henry Black's scarce 1830 edition exist outside the repositories searched?

next_steps: Collate each HI span against the complete IIIF manuscript bundle and the 1883 page image.
Locate an explicit typographic key in Horstmann's related editions, correspondence, or publisher matter.
Record expansions, supplied readings, manuscript corrections, and editorial conjectures separately if a machine-actionable collation is created.
notes: The available facsimile makes this research practical, but the present lineage does not equate every italic span with one kind of elision.
openQuestions.question: What exact editorial function does each of Horstmann's 6,738 italicized letter spans serve?

next_steps: Compare Dockray-Miller's full source apparatus with Horstmann's objections to identifying the named Legenda with the short Bollandist Vita.
Do not create a direct physical-exemplar relation without manuscript or edition evidence.
openQuestions.question: Which recension and physical exemplar of the Latin Edith legend underlies the Middle English poem?

next_steps: Ask licensing@bl.uk for an item-specific written determination, quoting the IIIF statement Public Domain in most countries other than the UK.
Keep the complete image bundle Git-ignored until redistribution rights are clear.
Separate use of public-domain medieval text and factual collation from republication of the image files.
openQuestions.question: What written reuse determination does the British Library apply to redistribution or publication of these IIIF images in the United Kingdom and in a commercial print-on-demand context?

next_steps: Use the parent record's current full-digital-coverage link and working IIIF manifest for access.
Retain the component's contrary sentence as historical catalog evidence until the British Library updates it.
notes: All 577 provider canvases were successfully cached and the ZIP passed integrity validation.
openQuestions.question: Are digital images of Cotton MS Faustina B III currently available despite the component catalog's legacy unavailability statement?

reviewNotes: Current British Library foliation governs discovery and IIIF navigation; Horstmann's old foliation remains attached to edition and XML milestones.
Horstmann's relation to Black is modeled as direct manuscript transcription plus collation, not as an edition copied from Black.
The abstract poem, the two adjacent Latin texts, the physical codex, historical editions, U-M encoding, repository artifact, and digital photographs remain separate layers.

CME00147 stops on printed p. 112 / current f. 264r after seven Auctoritates entries; the complete source list continues for seven more entries on p. 113 / f. 264v and is present in the full cached sources but not attributed to the encoding.
The complete codex and complete Horstmann volume are cached; work_portion locators associate the target without cropping the source objects.
The Benskin article cache is a supporting scholarly source with no open redistribution license located; it remains Git-ignored.
The British Library image-rights record preserves the provider's exact territorial caveat and does not convert it into a universal open license.

lineage.summary: The checked-in XML is a repository copy of University of Michigan item AJE0623.0001.001, internally identified as CME00147. It encodes Carl Horstmann's 1883 title page and printed pp. 1-112: the 4,986 surviving Middle English verse lines of the work now usually called The Wilton Chronicle, the complete Latin Nomina fundatorum, and only the first seven entries of the following Auctoritates. It omits the remainder of Auctoritates on printed p. 113 as well as Horstmann's German introduction and final remarks. Horstmann transcribed British Library Cotton MS Faustina B III directly and collated his readings against William Henry Black's 1830 first edition. The unique manuscript witness is now ff. 199r-263r for the poem and ff. 263r-264v for the complete adjacent Latin texts; the encoded selection ends on f. 264r. Horstmann's milestones use an older foliation five leaves lower. A complete 577-canvas British Library IIIF surrogate of the whole codex and a complete 136-page scan of Horstmann's volume are cached locally with fixity. The poem occupies current ff. 199r-263r / IIIF canvases 407-535, with the ending and Nomina fundatorum sharing f. 263r / canvas 535; the encoded Auctoritates opening ends on f. 264r / canvas 537. The British Library component record's legacy claim that images are unavailable conflicts with the current parent record and working IIIF service; the current public service governs access while the conflict remains documented.

catalogSummary.title: The Wilton Chronicle

catalogingSubject.label: The Wilton Chronicle
catalogingSubject.scope_note: One anonymous Middle English verse work composed for or in close association with the Benedictine convent at Wilton around 1420. It joins a history of Wilton and its royal founders to the life, relic translation, miracles, and cult of St Edith of Wilton. The sole witness is British Library Cotton MS Faustina B III, current ff. 199r-263r. CME00147 represents Carl Horstmann's 1883 edition of the 4,986 surviving verse lines and also preserves the complete adjacent Latin Nomina fundatorum plus the first seven of fourteen Auctoritates entries in the same manuscript hand. Those companion representation parts and Horstmann's German editorial frame do not redefine the abstract work as mixed-form or German-language content.

The remaining seven entries are present in Horstmann and the manuscript but absent from CME00147.

scope.description: Date assigned to the Cotton Faustina B III component containing the work.
First printed edition identified by Horstmann and DIMEV.

extent.description: One surviving verse work represented by 4,986 L elements in three LG wrappers. The wrappers reflect Horstmann/XML segmentation around a twelve-folio manuscript lacuna after line 1030 and a marked second part at line 2391; they are not three independent works. The CME item additionally contains the complete Latin Nomina fundatorum in 33 P elements and the opening Auctoritates paragraph with a seven-item LIST; seven further authorities on source p. 113 are omitted. Across TEXT it has 3 DIV1, 5 DIV2, 6 HEAD, 46 P, 113 PB, 4,986 L, 3 LG, 347 NOTE, 65 MILESTONE, 6,738 italic HI, 396 SUP, and 2 TAILNOTE elements.

formStatements.description: A continuous Middle English narrative poem of 4,986 surviving encoded lines, divided by the editor around a manuscript lacuna and a translation-of-relics heading.

scope.description: The abstract Wilton Chronicle.

assertion.notes: The label describes one major function and should not displace the work's hagiographic center.

scope.description: Chronologically organized history of Wilton, Wessex, royal founders, and the saint's cult integrated into the verse life.
Vernacular devotional and institutional verse intended for a religious community and its wider audience.

languageStatements.label: Middle English
notes: The extant copy has mixed dialect features. Benskin separates the likely South Wiltshire originals from a copyist associated linguistically with northern Warwickshire or southern Staffordshire and probably working at Wilton.

scope.description: The language of the 4,986 surviving verse lines.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Short Latin quotations also occur within the English verse.

scope.description: The two substantive prose appendices copied immediately after the poem and included in CME00147.

notes: The U-M catalog places St Edith in an author position, but she is the poem's subject rather than its author; that catalog convenience is not transferred to responsibility metadata.
The preferred title follows current scholarship. Chronicon Vilodunense and The Life of St Edith remain searchable alternates.
The poem is one work in one witness. The three LG wrappers are representation segments, not independent works.

The Latin addendum is medieval companion content in the same hand and is included because CME00147 encodes all of Nomina fundatorum and the opening half of Auctoritates. It remains separately scoped from the English verse work, and the omitted second half of Auctoritates is not implied by the part record.
The surviving manuscript copy is not treated as autograph. Benskin's dialect analysis requires separating likely original language, scribal language, place of composition, and place of copying.
Current foliation is used for discovery and facsimile navigation; Horstmann's old foliation is retained for reproducibility of his edition and the XML milestones.
A complete British Library IIIF bundle, not merely the target folios, is cached in the Git-ignored source-cache directory and linked through the lineage manifest.

Seek physical or codicological evidence for when the gathering was removed.
notes: Horstmann's approximately 980 missing lines is an estimate and is excluded from the surviving verse count.

next_steps: Collate CME00147's 6,738 italic HI spans against the complete IIIF manuscript images and the 1883 page images.

openQuestions.question: Which manuscript features does each of Horstmann's italicized letter sequence represent: abbreviation expansion, supplied text, correction, overwritten reading, or another editorial distinction?

assertion.notes: Composition at or for Wilton is strongly supported; the author's office, gender, and personal identity remain unknown.

scope.description: Likely place and institutional setting of composition.

assertion.notes: The extant manuscript language is mixed and predominantly West Midland; that scribal profile must not be flattened into the work's composition place.

scope.description: Benskin's reconstruction of the likely dialect of the work's originals.
Broad cultural origin and historical horizon.

assertion.notes: The author had close knowledge of Wilton and probably wrote for its female religious community, but no personal name or secure clerical status is known.

notes: Older suggestions that the poet was one of Wilton's chaplains or that the sole copy was autograph remain hypotheses, not established authorship.
Benskin distinguishes authorial language from the extant copyist's mixed dialect and rejects a simple autograph model.

summaries.value: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within a long account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, its royal patrons, and the abbey's institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, miracles associated with her shrine and relics, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its community. Its combination of hagiography and history constructs a usable past for a female religious house rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.

scope.description: Surviving work, complete Nomina fundatorum, and the opening half of Auctoritates represented by CME00147.

summaries.value: The poem opens with English and West Saxon history and Wilton's founders, moves into Edgar's encounter with Wulfthryth, and then loses an entire twelve-folio gathering after encoded line 1030. The surviving text resumes with Edith's upbringing, ascetic life, miracles, death, and first shrine cult. At line 2391 Horstmann marks a second part beginning with the translation of Edith's relics; later sections narrate Wulfthryth, royal patrons, punishments, healings, visions, and Edith's continuing protection of Wilton. Two Latin prose texts follow in the same manuscript hand: a list of Wilton founders through Henry V and a fourteen-entry list of authorities. CME00147 includes the founders text and only the first seven authority entries, ending on printed p. 112 / current f. 264r.

scope.description: Cataloging boundary among work, appendices, manuscript, edition, and encoding.

summaries.value: CME00147's root language attribute lists Middle English, Latin, and German because the encoding preserves the poem, a Latin companion selection, and Horstmann's German apparatus. Work-level form remains verse and its primary language remains Middle English. Horstmann's long German title belongs to the 1883 edition. Current British Library foliation is five leaves later than Horstmann's old milestones across this component: the poem is now ff. 199r-263r rather than ff. 194r-258r. The same current f. 263r and IIIF canvas 535 contain the poem's ending above and Nomina fundatorum below; the encoded selection ends on f. 264r / canvas 537 before the Auctoritates continuation on f. 264v / canvas 538.

assertion.notes: Current scholarship uses this concise title while recognizing that the work is both institutional history and hagiography.

scope.description: The abstract Middle English work.

notes: Horstmann argued that Edith's legend, rather than annalistic history alone, is the poem's central purpose.

scope.description: Descriptive title emphasizing the work's hagiographic center.

notes: This title should not imply that the text is merely a chronicle; its hagiographic and institutional-historical functions are inseparable.

scope.description: Historical title used by Leland, Black, and later scholarship.
First surviving verse line, with Horstmann's punctuation omitted.

work.summary: The Wilton Chronicle is an anonymous Middle English poem composed around 1420 for or in close association with Wilton Abbey. It frames the life and miracles of St Edith within a long account of Wessex, Wilton's foundation, its royal patrons, and the abbey's institutional memory. After Edith's life and death, the poem narrates the translation of her incorrupt body, miracles associated with her shrine and relics, episodes concerning her mother Wulfthryth, and later interventions protecting Wilton and its community. Its combination of hagiography and history constructs a usable past for a female religious house rather than functioning as a purely secular chronicle.
